content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Dianne Jacob 
Supervisor, 2nd District 
County of San Diego 
1600 Pacific Highway, Room 355 
San Diego, CA 92101 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-039 
Dear Ms. Jacob: 
March 23, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any 
conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair 
Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact. (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section l 090. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in, or voting on, a decision by the Board of 
Supervisors to authorize staff to enter into an Intergovernmental Agreement and Settlement 
("IGA") of a pending lawsuit involving the construction of a casino by the Jamul Indian Village? 
CONCLUSION 
It does not appear that the decision regarding the IGA will have a foreseeable material effect 
on your property interests. Barring additional facts, you are not prohibited from making or 
participating in the decision. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-16-039 
Page No. 2 
You are the San Diego County Supervisor for District No.2 and have held this office for 
twenty-three years. District No.2 consists of approximately 620,000 constituents and is a largely 
rural area spreading across 2,000 square miles of the southeastern portion of San Diego County. 
You and your husband currently own and reside in a home in Steele Canyon located in an 
unincorporated area of San Diego County known as Jamul, California. You and your husband also 
own rural property consisting of three contiguous parcels that we refer to as "the Ranch," which are 
also located in Jamul. Combined, the Ranch parcels total approximately 90 acres. 
The Jamul Indian Village ("JIV") is building a three-story 203,000 square foot casino on 
tribal land located along State Route 94, also located in Jamul. The proposed primary point of 
access for visitors to the casino will be via State Route 94. As the crow flies, your residence is 
approximately 2.8 miles from the casino. The shortest driving distance from your residence to the 
casino is approximately 4.1 miles. 
Your residence is located just off of J amul Drive on the north side of Jamul, the opposite 
side of the city as the casino. Jamul Drive runs parallel to State Route 94 on the other side of a ridge 
(approximately½ mile from Route 94). Your residence can be accessed from other more populated 
areas from Steele Canyon Road, without traversing Route 94. 
The Ranch property is approximately 6. 7 miles from the casino, as the crow flies, and the 
shortest driving distance to the casino from the Ranch property is approximately 9 miles. The 
Ranch is located off Honey Springs Road south of the casino. Honey Springs Road can be accessed 
from Jamal by State Route 94, Skyline Truck Trail, or Lyons Valley Road. The Ranch is several 
miles from the intersection of Honey Springs Road and State Route 94. 
On January 24, 2014, the County initiated litigation against the California Department of 
Transportation C'Caltrans'') and JIV alleging that Caltrans violated the California Environmental 
Quality Act ("CEQA ") by improperly segmenting its approval of an encroachment permit needed 
for the construction and operation of the casino. JIV demurred to the action and it was dismissed. 
Caltrans prevailed at the trial court and the matter is currently on appeal. 
Currently, the Board of Supervisors plans to consider whether to enter into the IGA with JIV 
that includes mitigation measures for off-reservation impacts of its casino. In return, the County 
would agree to dismiss the pending lawsuit; withdraw a Stop Work Order issued by the County 
against JIV; and agree to accept and transfer to Caltrans a County-owned irrevocable offer of 
dedication on SR94. 
If approved by the Board of Supervisors, the IGA would include the following provisions: 
• Hazardous Materials. Remediation procedures for contaminated soil or groundwater 
resulting from construction, and semi-annual reporting on hazardous material storage 
and use. 
• Biological Resources. Habitat monitoring and reporting. 


File No. A-16-039 
Page No. 3 
• Stonnwater Runoff. A bio-retention facility that wil1 retain and treat project-related 
runoff and semi-annual mitigation reports. 
• Visual Resources. All project lighting sources shall comply with the "Dark Sky 
Ordinance," and semi-annual mitigation reports on this measure. 
• Noise. An agreement to comply with the County's Noise Ordinance. 
• 
Air Quality. Implementation of measures during construction designed to reduce dust 
emissions. 
• Water Resources. Water supplied to the project shall come from the Otay Water District 
and not from groundwater and an agreement to prepare a discharge monitoring system, 
response plan, and biannual reports on the status and activities of the wastewater 
treatment system. 
• 
Sheriff Personnel. An agreement to provide the County $80,706 for startup costs, and 
$275,197 in annual payments plus addition overtime as necessary for a full-time deputy 
sheriff dedicated to the casino property. 
• Traffic. An agreement to negotiate in good faith at a future date to determine the 
casino's traffic impacts and the necessary funding to remediate those impacts. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Different standards 
apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material 
depending on the nature of the interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are 
defined in Section 87103. 
Under Sections 82033 and 87103(b ), an official has an interest in any real property owned 
directly, indirectly, or beneficially by the public official, or his or her immediate family, if the 
interest has a fair market value of $2,000 or more. Based upon the facts provided, the only interests 
identified that may be implicated by the decisions are your interests in real properties including 
your residence and the parcels that make up the Ranch.2 Accordingly, we must determine whether 
the financial effect from decisions regarding the IGA with the JIV on your real property interests, is 
both foreseeable and material. 
Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a 
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's 
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial 
2 Jeff Collins, ChiefofStaff, confirmed in telephone call on March 3, 2016, that you do not run a business 
from your residence or the Ranch. 


File No. A-16-039 
Page No. 4 
effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably 
foreseeable. (Regulation 1870l(b).) Different standards apply to detennine whether a reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. 
(Regulation 18702.) The materiality standards for any particular interest are provided in 
Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5. 
Based upon the facts provided, your properties are not explicitly involved in decisions 
regarding the IGA and a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable only if the effect can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. Additionally, for 
properties not explicitly involved in the decisions, Regulation l 8702.2(a) provides a list of 
circumstances under which a reasonably foreseeable effect on the interests is material. As relevant 
to your facts, a foreseeable effect will be material if the decisions: 
"(7) Would change the development potential of the parcel of real 
property; 
"(8) Would change the income producing potential of the parcel ofreal 
property .... 
"(9) Would change the highest and best use of the parcel of real property 
in which the official has a financial interest; 
"( 10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
[,r.J ... [ii] 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
Undoubtedly, proposed casinos can be particularly contentious matters and are often 
associated with a broad range of effects on neighboring properties including a change in 
development potential and altering traffic levels and intensity of use. It is very likely that properties 
near or adjacent to the proposed casino will be significantly affected by decisions regarding the IGA 
because they are located on a rural property just outside of the City of Jamul. It is also apparent that 
traffic levels on State Route 94 will be substantially affected. 
However, your residence is approximately 2.8 miles from the nearest boundaries of the 
proposed casino, or 4.1 using existing roads. Your residence is also located just off a street 
paralleling State Route 94 on the other side of a ridge, can be accessed from more populated areas 


File No. A-16-039 
Page No. 5 
without traversing State Route 94, and on the opposite side of the city as the casino. The properties 
that make up the Ranch are approximately 6.7 miles from the nearest boundaries of the proposed 
casino, or 9 miles using existing roads. Additionally, the Ranch is several miles off of State Route 
94, on which most of the traffic resulting from the casino will be concentrated. While the effects of 
the decision may theoretically extend to these distances, your properties are not within the vicinity 
in which there will be a foreseeable material effect. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By:ÆG. Lau 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Supervisors to authorize staff to enter into an Intergovernmental Agreement and Settlement 
("IGA") of a pending lawsuit involving the construction of a casino by the Jamul Indian Village?

It does not appear that the decision regarding the IGA will have a foreseeable material effect 
on your property interests. Barring additional facts, you are not prohibited from making or 
participating in the decision.

from your residence or the Ranch.
